title: Navigating Reorgs & Organizational Change
description: How Staff+ engineers navigate reorgs and organizational change at FAANG. Covers reorg patterns, maintaining influence through uncertainty, identifying new power structures, and strategic positioning. Reorgs happen every 12–18 months at FAANG — navigating them is essential.

description.value:
Reorgs at FAANG are not failures — they are how companies adapt. Unlike SAP where org structures stay stable for years (often tied to product lines or regional structures), FAANG changes frequently. Common triggers: (1) New product strategy — a VP decides to bet on AI/ML and reshuffles teams to align with the new priority. (2) Leadership changes — a new director reorganizes their org to match their management philosophy. (3) Performance issues — a team is underperforming and the organization reshuffles to address capability gaps. (4) Scaling pains — a team has grown from 10 to 100 engineers and needs to split into focused sub-teams.

The reorg frequency reality: FAANG engineers typically experience 2-3 reorgs in their first 2 years. Some are minor (reporting line changes, team name changes). Some are major (team moves to a different org, manager changes, project priorities shift). For SAP engineers accustomed to org stability, this is the hardest adjustment. The key mindset shift: a reorg is not about you. It is about the company adapting. Your job is to stay valuable regardless of the org chart.

SAP Bridge: In SAP consulting, reorgs happen when contracts change or account leadership shifts. The individual engineer is largely unaffected — they continue working on the same project. At FAANG, a reorg directly affects your daily work: new manager, new team, new projects, new priorities. The SAP engineer who treats reorgs like a consulting engagement change ('new client, same skills') will adapt faster than those who treat it as a personal setback.

sections.title: Types of Reorgs and How to Respond

description.value:
Each reorg pattern demands a different response. The Staff+ engineer recognizes the pattern and adjusts their strategy accordingly.

Team mergers: Two teams become one. The biggest risk is role overlap — if both teams had a Staff+ engineer, one may be redundant. Response: Differentiate through unique value. What do you know that the other Staff+ engineer doesn't? System-specific knowledge, stakeholder relationships, historical context. Offer to take on a new area that neither team owned before.

Team splits: One team becomes two. Creates leadership vacuums — new team leads are needed. Response: Position yourself as a bridge between the two new teams. Offer to own the architectural coherence across the split. This creates visibility and influence.

Reporting line changes: You report to a different manager, but your work stays the same. Response: Learn the new manager's communication style and priorities within the first 2 weeks. Schedule a 'startup' 1:1: 'Here's what I'm working on, here's how I like to communicate, here's what I need from you, what do you need from me?'

New leadership: A new VP or director reshapes the org. Response: Provide continuity and technical context without being defensive. The new leader needs to understand what exists, what works, what's fragile. Offer to give an architectural overview. Be the trusted source of truth.

Cross-org migration: Your entire team moves from one org to another. New stakeholders, new processes, new culture. Response: Build relationships from scratch. Treat it like a new job. Schedule introductory meetings with all new stakeholders. Learn the new org's meeting cadences, decision-making processes, and unwritten rules.

The rumor-to-announcement period is the most dangerous time for your influence. During this period (typically 2-6 weeks), people are anxious, information is scarce, and decisions are being made behind closed doors. Your response during this period determines your position after the reorg.

What to do during the rumor period: (1) Keep delivering. Your project doesn't stop because of a reorg. Ship code, attend meetings, review designs. Normalcy is a signal of stability. (2) Maintain relationships. Don't withdraw. Continue your regular 1:1s, mentoring sessions, and cross-team collaborations. (3) Stay off the gossip circuit. Don't speculate about who is going where. If someone asks, say 'I'm focused on delivering X. I trust leadership to make the right org decisions.' (4) Gather information quietly. Read org announcements, attend all-hands meetings, talk to your manager. But don't actively seek confidential information. (5) Update your brag document. Ensure your recent achievements are documented and visible. A reorg is a reset — your reputation goes with you only if people remember your impact.

What to avoid: (1) Don't panic-apply to other teams. Stability-seeking is natural, but switching teams during a reorg adds risk. Wait until the new org settles. (2) Don't complain publicly. Negative comments during uncertainty are remembered long after the reorg is complete. (3) Don't hoard information. If you know something that affects your team, share it appropriately. Being a trusted information conduit is valuable.

items.task: Map the new org chart within 48 hours of announcement
items.why: Understanding who reports to whom helps you identify new decision-makers and influencers

items.task: Find your new reporting line and skip-level
items.why: Schedule introductory 1:1s before they get busy with their own transition

items.task: Schedule 1:1s with new leadership this week
items.why: First impressions during reorgs are amplified — be proactive, not reactive

items.task: Document your projects, impact, and commitments
items.why: New leadership needs to understand what you own and what you've delivered

items.task: Identify changes in goals and priorities
items.why: What mattered before the reorg may no longer be relevant — realign quickly
Your stakeholders may have changed — rebuild those relationships
Reorgs create visibility opportunities — step up for the important projects
Don't neglect old relationships while building new ones — maintain both

items.task: Update your brag document with recent wins
items.why: Your work may be evaluated by people who don't know your history

items.task: Find a peer who has navigated this reorg type before
items.why: Peer guidance is often more practical than manager guidance during reorgs

description.value:
After a reorg, the formal org chart is visible within days, but the real influence network takes weeks or months to emerge. The formal chart shows who reports to whom. The influence network shows whose opinions shape decisions, who gets the best projects, and who has the ear of leadership.

Signals of real influence: (1) Who is invited to strategy meetings that aren't on the org chart? (2) Whose design docs get approved quickly vs who gets stuck in review loops? (3) Who is asked to present at the all-hands or engineering review? (4) Whose opinions are referenced by leadership: 'As Sarah mentioned in her design review...' (5) Who gets the first pick of new projects? (6) Who is consulted on decisions outside their formal scope?

How to build influence in the new structure: (1) Be useful. Solve problems that no one else is solving. Documentation, incident response, cross-team coordination. (2) Be reliable. Deliver what you承诺. In the chaos of a reorg, reliability is稀缺. (3) Be visible. Present your work, share your knowledge, write design docs. Influence requires visibility. (4) Be connected. Build relationships across the new org. Don't just focus on your new manager — build relationships with peers, skip-levels, and stakeholders.

Reorgs create Staff+ opportunities. The engineers who thrive during reorgs position themselves in specific roles that become more valuable during organizational change.

Be the anchor: Disrupted teams need stability. During a reorg, teams lose their technical direction as priorities shift and leaders change. The Staff+ engineer who maintains technical continuity becomes the anchor. 'While everyone is figuring out the new org, I will make sure we don't break the payment system.' This builds immense goodwill and trust.

Be the trusted partner: New leaders need honest technical insight. They don't know the system, the history, or the landmines. The Staff+ engineer who provides clear, unbiased technical context becomes the trusted partner: 'The payment module is fragile. It has 3 known issues documented here. The monitoring stack has a gap in credit card processing. Here is what I recommend for the first 90 days.' Trust built in the first 30 days of a new leadership relationship pays back for the next 12 months.

Be the visionary: Ambiguity favors those with a direction. When no one knows what to build next, the engineer with a clear, well-reasoned vision wins. 'The team has been fragmented across 3 areas. I propose we focus on payment reliability for Q3, which aligns with the company's revenue growth initiative. Here is a 1-page roadmap.' In the vacuum of a reorg, clarity is leadership.

Be the connector: Reorgs create silos as teams reorganize. The Staff+ engineer who maintains cross-team relationships becomes the connective tissue. 'Team A is working on the payment API, Team B is building the checkout flow. I will make sure their designs are consistent.' This is invisible but critical work.

Reorgs are emotionally taxing. Even when the outcome is positive, the uncertainty, loss of relationships, and change in daily routines takes a toll. Staff+ engineers need to manage their own emotional response while also supporting their teams through the transition.

The emotional cycle of a reorg: (1) Denial — 'This won't affect me. I'll just keep doing what I'm doing.' (2) Anxiety — 'What if I lose my project? My manager? My team?' (3) Acceptance — 'OK, this is happening. Let me understand the new reality.' (4) Exploration — 'What opportunities does this create? What new things can I learn?' (5) Commitment — 'I'm invested in the new direction. Let me make this work.' The Staff+ engineer moves through this cycle faster than their peers — ideally reaching acceptance within 1-2 weeks of the announcement.

Supporting your team through reorgs: (1) Acknowledge the uncertainty. Don't pretend everything is fine. 'I know this reorg is unsettling. I don't have all the answers, but here is what I know and here is when I will know more.' (2) Provide stability where you can. Keep regular 1:1s, maintain project commitments, preserve team rituals. (3) Advocate for your team. If the reorg creates unfair outcomes for your team members, speak up. Leadership may not be aware of the impact on individual contributors. (4) Model the behavior you want to see. If you are calm, proactive, and focused, your team will be too.

SAP Bridge: SAP organizational changes typically follow annual restructuring cycles or acquisition integrations. The pace is slower and the changes are more predictable. FAANG reorgs happen faster, with less warning, and with higher frequency. The SAP engineer who treats each reorg as a 'new project assignment' rather than a 'personal crisis' will adapt faster. The skills that made you valuable before the reorg — technical depth, reliability, communication — are still valuable after the reorg. The org chart changed, but your capabilities did not.

details: Staff+ Insight: FAANG reorgs every 12–18 months. This is not dysfunction — it is how companies adapt. Your technical knowledge is portable across orgs. Your relationships are not. The engineers who thrive use reorgs to reset, realign, and rebuild influence networks. For SAP engineers, the key insight is: treat each reorg like a new project engagement. You have done this before — new client, new team, new expectations. The difference is the client is your own company and the engagement is indefinite. Apply the same adaptability that made you successful in SAP consulting.
